patches.find:
4.1 The "9.2-Sigma" Dashboard: One Constant, Not Twenty Tests

The dome claims 20 independent physical domains converge at 9.2-sigma significance. In reality, 14 of those 20 share the same fitted constant (λ_g = 8,619 km) — they're one measurement counted fourteen times. Three more are known physics constants relabeled with dome terminology. Strip the double-counting and the statistical significance collapses.

The Live Power page presents 20 physical domains and claims they converge with 9.2σ aggregate significance (p = 1.2 × 10⁻²⁰). This would be extraordinary if the 20 domains were independent. They are not.

The dome model has one key fitted constant: the geomagnetic scale length λg = 8,619 km (and its companion, apex height H₀ = 8,537 km). This single constant was fitted to geomagnetic data — specifically, the model page states that H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) "reconciles three previously contradictory H measurements" (Schumann-derived ~9,500 km, Polaris geometric 4,750 km, parameterization 9,086 km). Fitting a two-parameter exponential (H₀, λg) to three data points is classical curve fitting with one degree of freedom. The resulting λg then appears in the 14 domains below — but it was not derived independently of them. Fourteen of the twenty domains feed this same constant into different equations. Testing whether a fitted constant reproduces the data it was fitted to is not a prediction — it is a tautology.

The issue is not that multiple equations share a constant — that is normal in physics (G, c, and ℏ all appear in many formulas). The difference is directionality. When G was measured from laboratory gravitational experiments, it then correctly predicted unrelated phenomena (planetary precession, gravitational lensing). λg was fitted to Schumann, geomagnetic, and gravity data, then "tested" against Schumann, geomagnetic, and gravity data. No measurement of λg from Domain X has produced a surprising, confirmed prediction in an unrelated Domain Y. There is no out-of-sample prediction.

Verifiability note: Our 20-domain classification below is based on the dome's Live Power page (live.html) as archived on 7 April 2026, with dynamic data from status_history.json timestamped 5 April 2026. The full page content — including per-domain formulas, falsification thresholds, claimed tolerances, and the cross-domain correlation matrix — is preserved in our repository at raw-text/08-live-power.txt. Readers can compare our classification against the dome's own published descriptions to verify that we have not mischaracterized any domain.

Group A: λg-Dependent Domains (14 of 20)

All 14 domains below use λg = 8,619 km and/or H₀ = 8,537 km. Because they share this fitted constant, they are not statistically independent.

#DomainShared ConstantGlobe Predicts Same?Problem
1Schumann Resonanceλg, H₀YES (f = c/2πR)Both models predict 7.83 Hz — non-discriminating.
2Tesla Longitudinal Freqλg, vaYES (derived from it)Tesla patent 787412 does not contain the cited formula f = c/(2D) (see WIN-001). The 11.78 Hz value is derived from Tesla's 0.08484 s measurement — a round-trip propagation time through a spherical Earth at ~1.57c, a known superluminal phase velocity in the Earth-ionosphere waveguide. The dome relabels this globe-confirming measurement as disc resonance.
3NMP Drift RateλgYES (WMM2025)Both models track the pole. Divergence testable ~2028+.
4Equatorial GravityλgYES (WGS84 — the mathematical model of Earth's shape used by GPS)Uses the observed value 9.7803 m/s² as input — circular.
5EM-Gravity Coupling (κ)κ, λgYES (standard physics: zero)Membach SG (the Membach superconducting gravimeter, one of the world's most sensitive gravity instruments) measured 0.0 μGal. Data favors globe.
6Schumann SuppressionH₀, λgYESBoth models predict suppression: geomagnetic storms increase D-layer conductivity, absorbing Schumann signals regardless of cavity shape. Non-discriminating.
7Roaring 40s AAOλgYESCorrelation claimed with no causal mechanism tested.
8Telluric CutoffλgN/AMT literature shows an attenuation valley, not a peak.
9Ionospheric D-layerH₀, λgYESThe D-layer exists at 60–90 km altitude. Both models place conductive boundaries there. The dome predicts it from firmament proximity; the globe from UV photoionization. A discriminating test would be: does D-layer height change with dome geometry vs. solar zenith angle? The data matches zenith angle (globe).
10Mascon GravityλgYES (GRACE)Gravity anomalies are mapped by GRACE/GOCE satellites using orbital perturbation — a method that only works if the satellites exist in orbit. The dome model has no mechanism for sub-surface mass concentrations; it matches the pattern by fitting λg to the same data.
11Solar Angular DiameterH₀, λgYESGlobe predicts near-constant 32 arcmin (±1.7% from orbital eccentricity). Dome geometry predicts 30% variation through the day as sun distance changes. Observed: constant. See Section 7.1.
12Daily Kp–SR SuppressionH₀, λgYESBoth models predict this correlation — non-discriminating.
13Solar Wind PressureλgYES (MHD)Both models predict that increased solar wind compresses the magnetic boundary. The dome calls it firmament flex; the globe calls it magnetopause compression. Both yield the same observable (Dst drop). Non-discriminating.
14Schumann Harmonic SplitH₀, λgYESThe harmonic splitting (e.g., 7.83 vs 8.0 Hz) arises from asymmetry in the resonant cavity. Both a dome (elliptical disc) and a globe (land/ocean conductivity contrast) produce asymmetry. The dome's split is fitted; the globe's is derived from measured conductivity maps. Non-discriminating.

Key column: "Globe Predicts Same?" — The question is not whether the globe model is older or more established. The question is whether any of these 14 observations produce a result the dome uniquely explains and the globe cannot. In 13 of 14 cases, both models predict the same observable — making them non-discriminating. In #2, Tesla's own measurement assumes a spherical Earth. In #8, the dome's prediction contradicts the data. None of these 14 domains distinguish the dome from the globe.

Group B: Known Constants Claimed as Predictions (3 of 20)

#DomainProblem
15Lunar Magnetic Tide (M2 = 12.42h)M2 = 12.42 hours is set by orbital mechanics (the Moon's period relative to a rotating Earth). Any model that places the Moon in a ~24.84-hour apparent cycle — dome or globe — gets this period. The dome doesn't predict 12.42h from its own geometry; it imports it. Matching a known constant is calibration, not prediction.
16Roaring 40s Wind SpeedOn the globe, the unbroken Southern Ocean and Coriolis deflection produce strong westerlies at 40–50°S — a quantitative prediction from atmospheric dynamics that matches observations. On a flat disc, air circulation would be radially symmetric from the center. The dome model offers no mechanism for why winds are strongest in a specific annular band; it observes the pattern and claims it.
17Polaris Excess (+0.27°)Polaris is 0.74° from the true celestial pole and precesses around it. Its apparent altitude varies with atmospheric refraction (~0.5° near the horizon, ~0.02–0.03° at typical Polaris elevations of 35–60°). The claimed +0.27° "excess" falls within the combined offset + refraction budget. Furthermore, the dome model's own OPEN-006 concedes a systematic altitude excess of +0.32° to +1.29° across latitudes — disagreeing with the +0.27° claimed here by up to a factor of 5. The dome's refraction formula n(r) cannot account for these discrepancies (see Section 1b).

Group C: Potentially Testable but Problematic (3 of 20)

#DomainProblem
18Aetheric SlipstreamBoth models predict eastbound advantage. Three discriminating tests: (1) Seasonal — asymmetry is ~62 min in winter, ~51 min in summer, matching jet stream strength cycle; dome predicts constant. (2) Equatorial — asymmetry vanishes near equator where jet stream is absent; dome predicts it everywhere. (3) Hemispheric — pattern varies by southern latitude, matching varying wind patterns. All three match jet stream, not fixed aether.
19GPS SagnacThe Sagnac correction in GPS is real and both models could claim it. The discriminating evidence: GPS requires relativistic corrections (+38.7 μs/day net — general relativity adds +45.9 μs/day from weaker gravity at altitude, special relativity subtracts 7.2 μs/day from orbital velocity; see Ashby 2003) calibrated to orbital altitude (20,200 km). These corrections are physical clock-rate adjustments that only produce the right positioning if the satellites are where orbital mechanics says they are. See Section 7.2 for the full three-leg argument.
20Eclipse 2026Pending. Dome registers dual baselines: BOU (−5 to −13 nT) and W004 (−10 to −26 nT) across 9 INTERMAGNET stations, spanning nearly the entire plausible quiet-day Chapman-mechanism range (5–25 nT). The BOU baseline predictions fall entirely within standard physics expectations. See Section 4.2 for full analysis including the four E-PRED sub-tests.

Bottom Line: One Ruler, Not Twenty Measurements

14 of 20 domains share the same fitted constant and are therefore one test, not fourteen. Of the remaining 6, the globe predicts the same or better in 5, and the 6th (Polaris) fails to discriminate — the claimed +0.27° excess falls within the error budget of atmospheric refraction, polar offset, and instrument precision, while the dome’s own OPEN-006 concedes systematic altitude excesses of +0.32° to +1.29° across latitudes (disagreeing with the +0.27° claimed here by up to a factor of 5). Zero of 20 domains produce a result where the globe disagrees and the dome uniquely explains the observation.

This structural analysis is confirmed by per-WIN code review (Part 2b): of {{CA_REVIEWED}} WINs reviewed, only {{CA_DOME}} derive their predictions from dome geometry; the remaining predictions are adopted from standard physics literature, renamed, and counted as confirmations. The dome's own OPEN-001 concedes it needs "dome-native coordinates without WGS84" — meaning its current coordinate system depends on the globe's. If the dome cannot locate cities without borrowing globe coordinates, the claim that its 20 domains are derived from first principles with locked constants is contradicted by the dome's own admission.

An analogy: imagine measuring your height with the same ruler in 14 rooms. You get "6 feet" every time. That is not 14 independent confirmations — it is one ruler used 14 times. The dome model's λg = 8,619 km is the ruler. It was fitted once to geomagnetic data. Every domain that uses it (14 of 20) is asking the same question: "does this fitted constant reproduce the data it was fitted to?" The answer is always yes. That is one curve fit applied fourteen times, and the 9.2σ figure is meaningless.
patches.replace:
4.1 The "28-Domain / 100%" Dashboard: One Ruler, Many Rooms

The dome's Live Predictive Power dashboard now reports "28 of 28 scored domains passing at 100.0%" (up from 20 in prior releases). The expansion adds labels, not independent evidence: fourteen of the twenty-eight are static re-references to prior WINs (predicted = observed = a fixed constant), five import globe / standard-model values (sidereal day, WGS84 gravity, ΛCDM Hubble constant), two are Selleri-vs-Lorentz framework recastings with no data content, and the remainder share the same handful of fitted constants. Section 2b.5 has the per-domain audit; this section focuses on the statistical-independence claim underlying the headline 9.2-sigma / 100% numbers.

The Live Power page presents 28 physical domains and reports 100.0% passing, with a 7-day average of 100.0% and a claimed 9.2σ aggregate significance (p = 1.2 × 10⁻²⁰). This would be extraordinary if the 28 domains were independent. They are not.

The dome's full formula layer is published in formula_runtime.json. It contains exactly six formulas (FORM-V51-001 through FORM-V51-006) and, together with the three master geometric constants (disc_radius = 20,015 km, sun_altitude = 5,733 km, moon_altitude = 2,534 km), rests on roughly ten independent numerical knobs. Twenty-eight labels cannot carry more independent information than the ten knobs they are derived from. For the per-domain breakdown — STATIC / BORROWED / TAUTOLOGY / THEORY-FRAMEWORK / LOOSE / DISCRIMINATING — see Section 2b.5.

The dome model has one key fitted constant for the 14 λg-dependent domains listed below: the geomagnetic scale length λg = 8,619 km (and its companion, apex height H₀ = 8,537 km). This single constant was fitted to geomagnetic data — specifically, the model page states that H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) "reconciles three previously contradictory H measurements" (Schumann-derived ~9,500 km, Polaris geometric 4,750 km, parameterization 9,086 km). Fitting a two-parameter exponential (H₀, λg) to three data points is classical curve fitting with one degree of freedom. The resulting λg then appears in the 14 domains below — but it was not derived independently of them. Fourteen of the twenty-eight scored domains feed this same constant into different equations. Testing whether a fitted constant reproduces the data it was fitted to is not a prediction — it is a tautology.

The issue is not that multiple equations share a constant — that is normal in physics (G, c, and ℏ all appear in many formulas). The difference is directionality. When G was measured from laboratory gravitational experiments, it then correctly predicted unrelated phenomena (planetary precession, gravitational lensing). λg was fitted to Schumann, geomagnetic, and gravity data, then "tested" against Schumann, geomagnetic, and gravity data. No measurement of λg from Domain X has produced a surprising, confirmed prediction in an unrelated Domain Y. There is no out-of-sample prediction.

Verifiability note: Our classification below tracks the dome's Live Power page (live.html) and daily_review.json as of 2026-04-17, plus the 28-domain roster recorded in the dome's own monitor.py and status_history.json. Detailed per-domain cross-links to dome source files are collected in Section 2b.5. The dome's own methodology.json (2026-04-17) states in plain language that "display ratio is bookkeeping, not a substitute for claim-by-claim audit"; we take him at his word and supply the audit.

Group A: λg-Dependent Domains (14 of 28)

All 14 domains below use λg = 8,619 km and/or H₀ = 8,537 km. Because they share this fitted constant, they are not statistically independent.

#DomainShared ConstantGlobe Predicts Same?Problem
1Schumann Resonanceλg, H₀YES (f = c/2πR)Both models predict 7.83 Hz — non-discriminating.
2Tesla Longitudinal Freqλg, vaYES (derived from it)Tesla patent 787412 does not contain the cited formula f = c/(2D) (see WIN-001). The 11.78 Hz value is derived from Tesla's 0.08484 s measurement — a round-trip propagation time through a spherical Earth at ~1.57c, a known superluminal phase velocity in the Earth-ionosphere waveguide. The dome relabels this globe-confirming measurement as disc resonance.
3NMP Drift RateλgYES (WMM2025)Both models track the pole. Current dashboard cell: predicted 22.2 km/yr, observed 37 km/yr — miss of 39.9% within a 50% tolerance window.
4Equatorial GravityλgYES (WGS84 — the mathematical model of Earth's shape used by GPS)Uses the observed value 9.7803 m/s² as input — circular.
5EM-Gravity Coupling (κ)κ, λgYES (standard physics: zero)Membach SG (the Membach superconducting gravimeter, one of the world's most sensitive gravity instruments) measured 0.0 μGal. Data favors globe. κ = 1.67 is itself FORM-V51-004, calibrated to BOU 2017 eclipse — the same event used as the test observable.
6Schumann SuppressionH₀, λgYESBoth models predict suppression: geomagnetic storms increase D-layer conductivity, absorbing Schumann signals regardless of cavity shape. Non-discriminating. (Currently reported as pending — requires Kp ≥ 4, which has not occurred in the measurement window.)
7Roaring 40s AAOλgYESCorrelation claimed with no causal mechanism tested. (Currently reported as pending — AAO data unavailable.)
8Telluric CutoffλgN/AMT literature shows an attenuation valley, not a peak. Static re-reference to WIN-008.
9Ionospheric D-layerH₀, λgYESThe D-layer exists at 60–90 km altitude. Both models place conductive boundaries there. The dome predicts it from firmament proximity; the globe from UV photoionization. A discriminating test would be: does D-layer height change with dome geometry vs. solar zenith angle? The data matches zenith angle (globe).
10Mascon GravityλgYES (GRACE)Gravity anomalies are mapped by GRACE/GOCE satellites using orbital perturbation — a method that only works if the satellites exist in orbit. The dome model has no mechanism for sub-surface mass concentrations; it matches the pattern by fitting λg to the same data.
11Solar Angular DiameterH₀, λgYESGlobe predicts near-constant 32 arcmin (±1.7% from orbital eccentricity). Dome geometry predicts 30% variation through the day as sun distance changes. Observed: constant. See Section 7.1.
12Daily Kp–SR SuppressionH₀, λgYESBoth models predict this correlation — non-discriminating. (Pending: no storm in window.)
13Solar Wind PressureλgYES (MHD)Both models predict that increased solar wind compresses the magnetic boundary. The dome calls it firmament flex; the globe calls it magnetopause compression. Both yield the same observable (Dst drop). Non-discriminating. (Currently pending.)
14Schumann Harmonic SplitH₀, λgYESThe harmonic splitting (e.g., 7.83 vs 8.0 Hz) arises from asymmetry in the resonant cavity. Both a dome (elliptical disc) and a globe (land/ocean conductivity contrast) produce asymmetry. The dome's split is fitted; the globe's is derived from measured conductivity maps. Non-discriminating. (Currently pending multi-station data.)

Key column: "Globe Predicts Same?" — The question is not whether the globe model is older or more established. The question is whether any of these 14 observations produce a result the dome uniquely explains and the globe cannot. In 13 of 14 cases, both models predict the same observable — making them non-discriminating. In #2, Tesla's own measurement assumes a spherical Earth. In #8, the dome's prediction contradicts the data. None of these 14 domains distinguish the dome from the globe.

Group B: Static Re-References to Prior WINs (14 of 28, NEW)

The April 2026 dashboard expansion from 20 to 28 domains was primarily achieved by adding static re-references to prior WINs. In each of the 14 entries below, the source field ends "(WIN-NNN static win)" or equivalent, and both predicted and observed are the same hardcoded literal. No fresh data flows through the check; the monitor reads two identical numbers from monitor.py and pronounces them equal. These cells cannot fail.

#DomainHardcoded checkPrior WIN
15SAA Decay Rate (TTB)77 = 77 nT/yr (20% tol)WIN-015
16Polaris Excess (35.9°N)0.27 = 0.27° (50% tol)WIN-001
17Lunar Magnetic Tide (M2)1.2 ± 0.5 = 1.5 nT (50% tol)WIN-039
18Ionospheric D-layer (live)85 = 83 km (5% tol)WIN-075
19Roaring 40s Wind Speed>20 = 22.5 m/s (25% tol)WIN-024
20Mascon Gravity Anomaly (live)~26 = 28.1 mGal (23% tol)WIN-076
21Solar Angular Diameter (live)0.53° = 0.531° (0.5% tol)WIN-056
22Lunar Phase H-component1–2 = 1.5 nT (50% tol)WIN-039 (second use)
23SAA–Roaring 40s Boundary47–50 = 48.5°S (2% tol)WIN-024 (second use)
24Crepuscular Ray Convergence5733 = 5733 km (10% tol)WIN-026
25Polaris Excess (45°N)0.36 = 0.36° (0.05% tol)WIN-065
26Moon Angular Diameter Variation11–14% = 12.5% (5% tol)OPEN-007
27Tesla Harmonic Series"Present" = "Present" (0% tol)WIN-001 (second use)
28GPS Clock Offset (Lorentz)"No" = "Confirmed" (0% tol)WIN-073

WIN-024, WIN-039, and WIN-001 each appear twice in the dashboard. Counting a single prior result as two independent "passes" is the purest form of label inflation. The entries in this group add zero independent evidence — they are citations to already-counted WINs, re-presented as live dashboard entries and re-counted toward the 28/28 headline.

Group C: Known Constants / Standard-Model Imports (treated as dome predictions)

DomainProblem
K1 Tidal Period (Sidereal Day)Predicted 23.9345 h = observed 23.9345 h (0.01% tol). The sidereal day is an IERS-published celestial-mechanics standard. Not derived from dome geometry.
S2 Solar Tidal Period12.00 h = 12.00 h (0.01% tol). Half of the solar day. True on any rotating geometry with a 24-hour day.
M2 Tidal Period12.42 h = 12.4206 h (0.1% tol). Standard harmonic, agnostic to Earth shape.
Equatorial Gravity9.832204 = 9.8322 m/s². The dome's formula g(r) = 9.7803·(1 + 0.005307·exp(−r/λG)) hardcodes the WGS84 International Gravity Formula coefficients 9.7803 and 0.005307. Predicted = WGS84 by construction.
Aetheric Redshift Scale λA4,283 = 4,319 Mpc (10% tol). λA is computed as c/H₀ with H₀ = 70 km/s/Mpc — the ΛCDM Hubble constant. The "aetheric" label is a rename of standard cosmology's inverse Hubble length.
P-wave Shadow Zone (104°–140°)"Shadow exists" vs "none found", directional. Asserting a classical seismology finding (liquid outer core signature) does not derive it from dome geometry.

Roaring 40s Wind Speed and Polaris Excess (+0.27°), discussed in our prior Group B, remain in the dashboard as static re-references. We have moved their analysis into Group B above.

Group D: Theory-Framework Recastings (2 of 28)

DomainProblem
GPS Clock Offset (Lorentz)Choosing between Selleri's absolute-simultaneity reformulation and Lorentz's relativity framework is a mathematical equivalence; GPS engineers obtain the same numbers either way. This is not a data test.
GPS Sagnac VerificationSame Selleri/Lorentz recasting. See Section 7.2 for the full three-leg argument. The claim does not separate the dome from the globe at the level of measurements.

Group E: Loose / Directional Checks (3 of 28)

DomainProblem
CMB Axis of Evil">2σ" vs "2.5σ". A well-documented Planck-2018 anomaly interpretable under many cosmologies. Passing a ">2σ" bar when the observed effect is 2.5σ is a mild directional check, not a discriminating prediction.
Aetheric Slipstream (JFK–LHR)Currently pending live flight data. Three discriminating tests defeat the fixed-aether claim: (1) Seasonal — asymmetry is ~62 min in winter, ~51 min in summer, matching jet stream strength cycle; dome predicts constant. (2) Equatorial — asymmetry vanishes near equator where jet stream is absent; dome predicts it everywhere. (3) Hemispheric — pattern varies by southern latitude, matching varying wind patterns. All three match jet stream, not fixed aether.
Eclipse Magnetic Anomaly (Aug 12 2026)Pending. Dome registers dual baselines: BOU (−5 to −13 nT) and W004 (−10 to −26 nT) across 9 INTERMAGNET stations, spanning nearly the entire plausible quiet-day Chapman-mechanism range (5–25 nT). The BOU baseline predictions fall entirely within standard physics expectations. See Section 4.2 for full analysis including the four E-PRED sub-tests.

Genuinely discriminating cells (roughly 1 of 28)

After the static re-references, borrowed standard values, tautologies, theory-framework recastings, and loose directional checks are removed, the 28-of-28 passing list reduces to roughly one or two cells with real forward-discriminating content: NMP Longitude (within 1% of observed, 5% tol) and, at best, Aetheric Redshift Scale (which internally uses the borrowed Hubble constant). The remaining "genuine" forward discriminator on the site is the pred-eclipse-tier3 prediction — and by the author's own methodology.json, that is prospective, not confirmed.

Bottom Line: One Ruler, Many Rooms (updated for 28)

14 of 28 domains are static re-references to prior WINs — hardcoded checks where predicted equals observed by construction. A further 14 of 28 are λg-dependent — they share a single fitted constant and therefore are not statistically independent. Of the remaining cells, 5 import standard-model or globe-model values, 2 are Selleri / Lorentz framework recastings, and 3 are loose directional checks. When this classification is performed, the "100% / 28 of 28" headline reduces to roughly 1 genuinely-discriminating cell, and the 9.2σ figure collapses from "extraordinary convergence" to "many labels wrapped around the same handful of knobs."

This structural analysis is confirmed by per-WIN code review (Part 2b): of {{CA_REVIEWED}} WINs reviewed, only {{CA_DOME}} derive their predictions from dome geometry; the remaining predictions are adopted from standard physics literature, renamed, and counted as confirmations. The dome's own OPEN-001 concedes it needs "dome-native coordinates without WGS84" — meaning its current coordinate system depends on the globe's. If the dome cannot locate cities without borrowing globe coordinates, the claim that its 28 domains are derived from first principles with locked constants is contradicted by the dome's own admission.

An analogy: imagine measuring your height with the same ruler in 14 rooms. You get "6 feet" every time. That is not 14 independent confirmations — it is one ruler used 14 times. Then you add 14 more rooms in which you re-read the mark already written on the wall from a previous measurement, and pronounce yourself "6 feet" in those rooms too. The dome model's λg = 8,619 km is the ruler. It was fitted once to geomagnetic data. Fourteen of the twenty-eight scored domains use it, and another fourteen simply re-read prior numbers. The 9.2σ aggregate significance figure, computed as if the 28 domains were independent, is an artifact of the counting procedure. The dome author's own methodology.json says as much: "Display ratio is bookkeeping, not a substitute for claim-by-claim audit." This section is the audit.
patches.rationale: EXP-176 integration: replace stale 20-domain Part 4.1 with 28-domain One Ruler, Many Rooms. Adds Groups B/C/D/E, cross-refs SEC-2b.5, preserves {{CA_REVIEWED}}/{{CA_DOME}}.
